data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Bloom Energy Q1 smashes earnings and revenue estimates, lifts FY26 guidance
TWO Harbors agrees to CrossCountry's increased offer of $11.30/share
data.description:
Two Harbors Investment (TWO) agreed to an amended merger agreement with CrossCountry Mortgage, increasing the all-cash deal to $11.30 a share from $10.80. 

The amended deal comes after TWO Board’s "thorough evaluation" of an unsolicited competing proposal received on April 20 from mortgage wholesale UWM Holdings (UWMC), according to a  statement on Tuesday.

“Our increased bid reflects our continued excitement for this transaction and our strong conviction in the strategic and financial merits of combining CCM and TWO Harbors,” said Ron Leonhardt, Founder and CEO of CrossCountry Mortgage

The special meeting of shareholders for the transaction is set for May 19. The transaction is expected to close in the third quarter.

Houlihan Lokey is acting as financial advisor and Jones Day is acting as legal counsel to TWO. Citi is acting as exclusive financial advisor and Simpson Thacher & Bartlett is acting as legal counsel to CCM.

data.title: Purple Innovation updates 2026 revenue outlook to $465M-$485M while maintaining $20M-$30M adjusted EBITDA guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Purple Innovation (PRPL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We entered 2026 building on the progress we made in the fourth quarter, and our first quarter reflects continued progress and greater consistency across our channels" (CEO Robert DeMartini), adding, "We're entering the second quarter with improving trends and are positioned for a step-up in performance."
  • "In the first quarter, total sales were down 8%" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "E-commerce trends improved sequentially, declining 10% in the first quarter compared with down 15% in the prior period," while noting, "Showroom performance remained a bright spot with sales up 5% and comps up 7%, marking our third consecutive quarter of positive comp growth."
  • "Turning to our outlook. We're updating our revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million from the prior range of $500 million to $520 million due to the accounting-related adjustment" (CEO DeMartini), while stating, "We're maintaining our adjusted EBITDA guidance of $20 million to $30 million."
  • "Net revenue for the first quarter was $95.7 million, down 8.1% year-over-year" (CFO Todd Vogensen), and he added, "Excluding this accounting-related impact, net revenue would have been $100.6 million or down 3.4% year-over-year."
  • "Todd... will be stepping down as CFO effective May 1" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "We're also pleased to welcome Bob Lucian as our next CFO."

Outlook

  • "We're updating our full year revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million from the prior range of $500 million to $520 million due to the accounting-related adjustment discussed earlier" (CFO Vogensen), while reiterating, "We are maintaining our adjusted EBITDA guidance in the range of $20 million to $30 million."
  • "The impact to Q1 was only $5 million" (CFO Vogensen), and he said, "You can see that we adjusted the revenue guidance by about $35 million."
  • Compared with Q4 2025, "For the full year, we expect revenue in the range of $500 million to $520 million and adjusted EBITDA of $20 million to $30 million" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 guidance language changed to: "We're updating our revenue guidance" to $465 million to $485 million "due to the accounting-related adjustment" (CEO DeMartini, Q1 2026).

Financial Results

  • "The accounting-related reclassification had no impact on gross profit dollars, EBITDA or cash flow, but it reduced net revenues and cost of sales by the same amount" (CFO Vogensen).
  • "Gross margin for the quarter was approximately 36.8%" (CFO Vogensen), and he attributed the rate to Royale floor models and manufacturing overhead, adding, "As production and shipments normalize, we expect gross margin to return to approximately 40% by the second half of the year."
  • "Operating expenses in the quarter were $52 million, down 6.3% versus $55.5 million last year" (CFO Vogensen), while noting the quarter included "higher spend related to the ongoing evaluation of strategic alternatives."
  • "Our first quarter adjusted loss per share was $0.13 compared to an adjusted loss per share of $0.11 last year" (CFO Vogensen), and "Adjusted EBITDA in the first quarter was negative $4.8 million" (CFO Vogensen).
  • "We ended the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $25 million versus $24.3 million on December 31, 2025" (CFO Vogensen), and he called it "the best first quarter cash performance in 7 years" while adding, "Net inventories on March 31, 2026, were $58.1 million, down 2.7% compared to December 31, 2025."

Q&A

  • Taylor Zick (for Brad Thomas), KeyBanc: Can you discuss Q1 demand trends and why you’re confident in a Q2 step-up? CEO DeMartini: "January was fairly healthy. February got a little bit of choppy and then March got a little bit better across all channels," adding, "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now... the category is not robust."
  • Taylor Zick (for Brad Thomas), KeyBanc: What are you seeing in input costs and transportation pressures? CFO Vogensen: "we are seeing pressure across transportation as well as some of our input costs, including foam," but said those headwinds are "being roughly offset with savings that we're seeing on tariffs" and that the company should manage "within our guidance" if oil stays "around that $100 a barrel range."
  • Daniel Silverstein, UBS: Is the sales guidance change underlying demand or just reporting, and how is wholesale trending excluding new doors? CFO Vogensen: "it is purely just the reporting change" and "no change to the underlying activity"; CEO DeMartini said "our top 8 accounts, including Costco, Mattress Firm... are performing up year-on-year" but added, "we've got some smaller accounts that we got to figure out how to service better."
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH: What are you doing to sustain e-commerce improvement after March was flat? CEO DeMartini: "I don't know if it's too early... to call 1 month of trend," adding, "We've enrolled an outside agency... the early signals are good, but I'm not going to wave any success flag yet."
  • Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer: Will the accounting change continue in future quarters, and what sized the Q1 gross margin headwinds? CFO Vogensen: "it will be ongoing" and "if anything, the adjustment gets bigger"; on margin, "The impact from the floor models was about 200 basis points" and lower absorption was "close to 200 basis points of drag."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on accounting optics and sustainability, including "is there going to be a similar type impact in subsequent quarters?" (Brian Nagel, Oppenheimer) and "is anything changing from an underlying demand perspective or it's just a reporting adjustment?" (Daniel Silverstein, UBS).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, including "we're positioned for a step-up in performance" (CEO Robert DeMartini) alongside "I don't know if it's too early... to call 1 month of trend" (CEO DeMartini) and "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now" (CEO DeMartini).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management’s framing remained execution-led ("not by a recovery in the broader market" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025)), but Q1 2026 introduced more sensitivity to reporting and input costs, including "purely just the reporting change" (CFO Todd Vogensen, Q1 2026) and "we are seeing pressure" in foam and transportation (CFO Vogensen).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted primarily in reported revenue: "we expect revenue in the range of $500 million to $520 million" (CEO Robert DeMartini, Q4 2025) changed to "updating our revenue guidance to a range of $465 million to $485 million" (CEO DeMartini, Q1 2026), while adjusted EBITDA guidance stayed "$20 million to $30 million" in both quarters (CEO DeMartini).
  • Gross margin commentary became more transitional in Q1: Q4 described "gross margin... approximately 41.9%" (CFO Todd Vogensen, Q4 2025), while Q1 reported "approximately 36.8%" and tied it to "Royale floor models" and overhead absorption, with an expectation to return "to approximately 40% by the second half of the year" (CFO Vogensen, Q1 2026).
  • Management changes emerged in Q1 with "stepping down as CFO effective May 1" and naming "Bob Lucian as our next CFO" (CEO Robert DeMartini), which was not present in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • "we are seeing pressure across transportation as well as some of our input costs, including foam" (CFO Todd Vogensen), and he said the company is mitigating with tariff savings and sourcing work, adding, "we should be able to manage within our guidance."
  • "Gross margins came in below our normal 40% baseline" due to "higher levels of floor model discounts" tied to the Purple Royale rollout (CEO Robert DeMartini), which management characterized as "temporary" and expected to normalize.
  • "the consumer still is pretty nervous right now" (CEO DeMartini), and he said, "the category is not robust," framing demand as still uncertain.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as continued operational progress with channel mix shifting toward showrooms and premium products, while reported revenue and margin were pressured by an ongoing accounting-related wholesale reclassification and temporary Purple Royale floor-model dynamics. The company lowered 2026 reported revenue guidance to $465 million to $485 million for the accounting change while maintaining adjusted EBITDA guidance of $20 million to $30 million, and it outlined mitigation actions on input costs alongside a CFO transition from Todd Vogensen to Bob Lucian.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Arcbest expects 400 to 500 bps sequential OR improvement in Q2 as it launches ArcBest View in May
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ArcBest (ARCB) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter brought a challenging operating environment with severe winter weather, higher fuel prices and continued uncertainty" (President, CEO & Director Seth Runser), while he also said, "Customer demand has remained steady, and we continue to see improvement in our pipeline."
  • "Against that backdrop, we will launch ArcBest View in May" (President, CEO & Director Runser), describing it as a platform that "enables customers to quote, book and track shipments across our logistics solutions through a single intuitive interface" and adding that "early feedback has been very encouraging."
  • "Deferred price increases averaged 6% in the first quarter, our strongest result since the third quarter of 2022" (President, CEO & Director Runser), and he tied pricing control to mix tools: "the expansion of our dynamic quote pool has given us greater ability to make real-time pricing decisions, allowing us to be more selective and further optimize yield and profitability."
  • "To date, these efforts have generated $32 million in annualized cost savings" (President, CEO & Director Runser) from continuous improvement work, and he added on city route optimization: "To date, the program has delivered $15 million in annualized savings while also improving network efficiency and service."
  • "On a consolidated basis, first quarter revenue was $1 billion, up 3% year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Matt Beasley), and he added, "Non-GAAP operating income was $13 million" and "adjusted earnings per share were $0.32."

Outlook

  • "Historically, ABF's non-GAAP operating ratio improved by approximately 350 basis points from the first quarter to the second quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), and he guided: "Based on current trends, we expect second quarter performance to improve sequentially by approximately 400 to 500 basis points."
  • "Looking ahead, we expect second quarter non-GAAP operating income in Asset-Light to be in the range of approximately $1 million to $3 million" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), adding the range "reflects continued yield discipline, active cost management and improved productivity performance."
  • Compared with the prior call’s seasonality framing, management moved from "we currently expect our first quarter operating ratio to increase by approximately 100 to 200 basis points sequentially" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley, Q4 call) to an explicit second-quarter improvement range of "approximately 400 to 500 basis points" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley, Q1 call).

Financial results

  • "First quarter revenue was $655 million" in Asset-Based (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), and he said "ABF's operating ratio was 97.3%" alongside volume/mix drivers: "Daily tonnage increased 7%, reflecting a 2% increase in shipments per day and a 5% increase in weight per shipment."
  • "Operating expenses increased for several reasons" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), citing "additional labor needed to support shipment growth," "annual contract increases in union wage rates," "higher fuel prices" and "increased depreciation expense associated with our equipment investments."
  • In Asset-Light, "First quarter revenue was $378 million" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), and he emphasized productivity and cost performance: "Selling, general and administrative expense per shipment declined 15% to the lowest level on record" and "shipments per person per day increasing 26%," which he said contributed to "non-GAAP operating income of $3 million."
  • On capital returns and balance sheet, "In the first quarter of 2026, we returned more than $10 million through a combination of share repurchases and dividends" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley), and he added, "We have ample liquidity and a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio that is well below the S&P 500 average."

Q&A

  • Ravi Shanker, Morgan Stanley: asked about management’s comment that conditions are "becoming more constructive"; President, CEO & Director Runser answered, "Manufacturing/ housing continues to pressure our volumes" but added, "Capacity fundamentals continue to move in a more constructive direction" and "PMI move into expansion territory these past 3 months."
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: questioned truckload-to-LTL spillover and regulatory effects; President, CEO & Director Runser said, "spot rates are currently exceeding contract by 15% to 20%" and added, "we expect as we move through second and third quarter, it's probably going to be in the low to mid-double-digit range" for contract increases; Eddie Sorg added, "we have seen a tighter truckload capacity market" and "some early signs of business push into our integrated logistics solutions and LTL."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: asked what drives the Q2 OR improvement and fuel mechanics; Chief Financial Officer Beasley said, "it's across the board where we're seeing outperformance" and added, "The fuel changes are not the primary driver of the outperformance in the second quarter."
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: raised broker liability/safety headlines; President, CEO & Director Runser said, "Safety has really always been fundamentally how ArcBest operates" and "we use a structured compliance-based process to select and monitor third-party carriers," adding, "at this time, we don't expect these developments to change our outlook or our approach."
  • Ariel Rosa, Citigroup: asked how Runser’s approach differs from his predecessor; President, CEO & Director Runser said, "my goal is really to accelerate that progress we've already made" and highlighted "optimizing our sales resources" and that "when ArcBest View launches in May, it's really going to be differentiated in the marketplace."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, pressing on visibility into demand recovery, fuel lag, mix/weight drivers, and legal/regulatory risk (e.g., Wolfe’s focus on whether guidance was "driving" OR improvement and JPMorgan’s liability scenario setup).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks, and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing discipline and uncertainty (e.g., "the timing of the demand inflection still remains uncertain" (President, CEO & Director Runser) and "The fuel changes are not the primary driver" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley)).
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call used more “constructive” framing ("conditions are becoming more constructive" (President, CEO & Director Runser)) while the prior quarter repeatedly referenced a "prolonged freight recession" (President, CEO & Director Runser, Q4 call).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategy and product emphasis shifted from broad AI highlights to a near-term commercial platform milestone: Q1 introduced "we will launch ArcBest View in May" (President, CEO & Director Runser) after Q4 positioned AI as "a pivotal year for accelerating AI across our organization" (President, CEO & Director Runser, Q4 call).
  • Cost/efficiency progress was updated: Q1 said, "Continuous improvement training has now been implemented across approximately 75% of the network" and "generated $32 million in annualized cost savings" (President, CEO & Director Runser) versus Q4’s "implemented across approximately 60% of the network" and "delivered $24 million in annual cost savings" (President, CEO & Director Runser, Q4 call).
  • Financial tone tightened around Asset-Light: Q4 guided "an operating loss of up to $1 million" for Q1 (Chief Financial Officer Beasley, Q4 call), while Q1 reported "non-GAAP operating income of $3 million" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley) and guided Q2 Asset-Light profit of "$1 million to $3 million" (Chief Financial Officer Beasley).

Risks and concerns

  • "Severe winter weather" and "higher fuel prices" were cited as key near-term pressures (President, CEO & Director Runser), while the CFO warned that "periods of rapid fuel price movement can create short-term timing differences" between surcharge revenue recognition and incurred costs (Chief Financial Officer Beasley).
  • End-market sensitivity remained in focus: "Manufacturing/ housing continues to pressure our volumes" (President, CEO & Director Runser), and he added that housing-related demand affects U-Pack: "it's resulted in fewer household good moves" (President, CEO & Director Runser).
  • Regulatory/legal uncertainty surfaced in truckload brokerage, with management emphasizing mitigation through controls: "carriers that don't meet those requirements are not eligible to move freight for us" (President, CEO & Director Runser).

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as weather- and fuel-challenged but pointed to steadier demand, an improving pipeline, and early truckload tightening signals, while highlighting the May launch of ArcBest View, continued pricing discipline (including 6% deferred increases), and expanding productivity programs. The company guided to a 400 to 500 basis-point sequential improvement in the Asset-Based operating ratio in Q2 and expected Asset-Light Q2 operating income of $1 million to $3 million, while reiterating that fuel volatility can create timing impacts even with surcharge mechanisms.

data.title: The Bank of N.T. Butterfield & Son Non-GAAP EPS of $1.55 beats by $0.15, revenue of $154.5M beats by $4.56M
data.description:
• The Bank of N.T. Butterfield & Son press release (NTB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.55 beats by $0.15.
  • Revenue of $154.5M (+4.3% Y/Y) beats by $4.56M.
  • Return on average common equity of 22.1% and core return on average tangible common equity1 of 24.1%
